summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's compliance with the Epic v. Apple anti-steering injunction, which allows apps to link to external web purchases but requires a 27% commission on resulting sales within 7 days. He argues Apple's approach is likely legally compliant but strategically foolish, as it antagonizes regulators and damages Apple's brand reputation. Gruber distinguishes between games (where platform commissions are industry-standard) and other apps, arguing non-game apps should be allowed to direct users to web purchases without commission. He contends that even if Apple's moves are purely profit-motivated, the revenue gained isn't worth the regulatory and reputational costs. The piece also addresses the broader sideloading debate, acknowledging both the 'it's my device' argument and the genuine security benefits of iOS's closed ecosystem.
key_points: Apple's anti-steering compliance only charges commission on web sales initiated through in-app links within 7 days, not all web sales — making Sweeney's characterization misleading
Gruber believes Apple's compliance is likely legal but strategically wrong, trading short-term revenue for long-term regulatory antagonism
Games should be treated differently from other apps because hefty platform commissions are standard in the gaming industry — iPhones are consoles, not PCs
Apple should be seeking to placate regulators rather than adopting a confrontational 'bring it on' stance that invites harsher legislation
The iOS closed ecosystem genuinely protects typical users from adversarial software, even if Apple's motivations are primarily financial
money_quotes: Whatever revenue Apple would lose to non-commissioned web sales (for non-games) is not worth the hit they are taking to the company's brand and reputation — this move reeks of greed and avarice.
Rather than take a sure win with most of what they could want, Apple is seemingly hell-bent on trying to keep everything.
No matter what the sport, no matter what the letter of the rulebook says, it's never a good idea to piss off the refs.
The iPhone and iPad are not PCs; they're consoles for games and apps.
I only believe Apple shouldn't, not that they legally can't.
It is undeniable that adversarial software is not a problem for any typical users on iOS.
The iPhone is the converse: designed first and foremost for the non-savvy user, and tries to accommodate power users as best it can within the limits of that primary directive.
Apple seemingly wants to capture every last piece on the board — even while playing in a tournament where the referees (regulators) are known to look askance at blatant poor sportsmanship.
key_insight: Apple's legally defensible but maximally aggressive approach to App Store commission compliance is a strategic blunder that prioritizes short-term revenue over the far greater long-term cost of provoking regulators and eroding the company's reputation.
